[JS Weird World] No.28 A Quick Reminder: Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference

We're going to dig into one of the things you'll bump into constantly in JavaScript — pass by value and pass by reference. It's a really important concept, and if it's fuzzy in your head, it'll come back to bite you later during development and debugging.
Pass by value is actually pretty simple. Let's understand it through an example.

goes and finds the memory address of

and gets a brand new memory address. That whole process is what we call pass by value.
This one shows up a lot more often. If you have an object in JavaScript, assigning a variable to that object also gives you a memory address. But when you say
, what you're really saying is "make these two things the same." So
does NOT get a new memory address. That's pass by reference.
Let's look at a pass-by-value example first.

First, let's set up an object example.

That's pass by reference, and the
step is what we call mutate.
So when the assignment operator knows you want to change some value, it goes and mutates it.
Mutate just means "change something." When we say "I mutate my object," it means I'm going to change it (the object). So here's the thing to take away: no matter which line we make the change on,
ends up changed too, because they both point to the same memory.
See the Pen by reference by HF (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
